summary: I was born in Port Angeles, Washington, which used to be a milltown when I was growing up. My early childhood there was shaped by the working-class environment, and I helped my father with various jobs around the house. Although the mills have since closed, those early experiences in Port Angeles laid the foundation for my understanding of labor and community.

When I was about eleven, I lived away from home for the summer and worked on a charter fishing boat at La Push, Washington. That summer was probably the best of my life, giving me a taste of independence and hard work in a beautiful coastal setting. It was one of my first real jobs and an important experience in my youth.

I started reading philosophy when I was still in grade school, and Plato was a big influence on me. Through him, I learned about the Socratic method, which shaped my thinking and approach to questions. His ideas stayed with me as I grew older and became more politically active.

Alongside Plato, I learned about Socrates and the Socratic method during my early readings in philosophy. This method of questioning helped me develop critical thinking skills that were useful later in debates and activism. It was part of the foundation that shaped my approach to ideas and discussions.

I read Karl Marx early on, even in grade school, which influenced my political views significantly. When I was in junior high during the early 1960s, I even debated in favor of Communism against capitalism in school. Marx's ideas helped me understand class struggles and motivated my anti-war and labor activism later in life.

The Vietnam War was a backdrop to much of my youth and early activism. In the early 1960s, as the war heated up, I became involved in anti-war organizing, including distributing literature and participating in protests. It was a defining issue that connected me with many others and shaped my political commitments.

entity: West Coast Pulp and Paper Workers Union
summary: I had a connection to the West Coast Pulp and Paper Workers Union through a girlfriend’s mother who worked there. This union was breaking away from their international union at the time, and I used their old mimeograph machine to print an anti-war newsletter. It was an early experience in labor activism and organizing.

The Cambodian Campaign in 1970 was a pivotal moment for me and my friend in our anti-war organizing. The following year, in 1971, we traveled around the country collecting petitions for the People's Peace Treaty and went to Washington, D.C. for a month of protests against the war. It was a time of intense political activity and community building.

In 1971, I spent a month in Washington, D.C. participating in anti-war protests and helping provide housing for demonstrators. We housed about a million people that month, and I witnessed a vibrant political community working together. I was arrested in Mount Vernon Square during a mass protest where 14,000 people were arrested in one day.

During the 1971 anti-war protests in Washington, D.C., I was arrested in Mount Vernon Square along with thousands of others. We were held in a small fenced park and later in a football field surrounded by barbed wire and National Guard troops. It was a tense but memorable experience, especially when local black leaders brought us fried chicken as a gesture of solidarity.

During the 1971 anti-war protests in Washington, D.C., the National Guard was deployed to contain demonstrators, including myself. We were held in a football field surrounded by barbed wire and troops, and at one point, they even used tear gas on us. Their presence was intimidating but also highlighted the seriousness of the protest movement.

After a serious injury working in a lumber mill, I moved to Philadelphia to join a pacifist community inspired by Quaker ideals. However, I found the community's radical lifestyles didn't match my blue-collar background, so I eventually returned to school. Philadelphia was an important chapter in my life where I explored pacifism and alternative politics.

I was inspired by a pacifist who was a founding member of a Quaker peace community in Philadelphia, which influenced my decision to join their community after my injury. Although the experience was somewhat disappointing due to lifestyle differences, the Quaker ideals of peace and pacifism had a lasting impact on me. Their approach contrasted with my more blue-collar, practical politics.

I was awarded a scholarship in chemical engineering to the University of Washington, but unfortunately, when I arrived to collect it, the scholarship had been given away. Despite this setback, I later found a job at Boeing and continued my education and career in science and engineering. The University of Washington was part of my path even though I didn't attend as planned.

I worked at Boeing for 25 years, starting without a degree but with a strong recommendation. Boeing was an old-time American company that valued what you could do, and I was able to work on projects like the Hubble Space Telescope and environmental monitoring. It was a good career that allowed me to grow professionally and contribute to important aerospace work.

The company I worked for at Boeing no longer exists due to the Boeing-McDonnell Douglas merger. This corporate change marked the end of an era for the workplace I knew. The merger affected many workers and the aerospace industry in Seattle during my career.

One of the projects I was proud to work on at Boeing was the Hubble Space Telescope. I was the first employee in their environmental laboratory monitoring effluents from their sites, which was part of the broader work supporting aerospace missions like Hubble. This work combined my interests in science and engineering with important space exploration efforts.

In my forensic work, I once investigated a case involving an executive who was thought to have been poisoned in a hotel room in Mongolia. By analyzing a dust sample, I determined the cause was insecticide sprayed by hotel staff, not poisoning. This case was an example of how my work took me around the world and involved complex scientific detective work.

I had the opportunity to conduct experiments on the Mir space station as part of my forensic and scientific work. This was a unique and satisfying experience that broadened my perspective on science and technology. Working with space station projects was a highlight of my career.

I worked as a forensic scientist studying diatoms in Puget Sound, which was part of my broader scientific investigations. This work connected me to the local environment and helped solve complex problems by analyzing natural samples. Puget Sound was a significant place for both my early life and professional work.
The transcript does not mention New Orleans in my story, so I have no personal connection or experience to share about that place.
The transcript does not include any mention of Harry Belafonte, so I do not have a personal story or connection involving him.
I did not mention Peter, Paul and Mary in my oral history, so I have no direct experience or relationship with this group to share.

While in Washington, D.C. during the 1971 anti-war protests, I was part of a large movement that gathered near the Capitol Building. The protests involved thousands of people and culminated in mass arrests, including mine. The Capitol was a symbolic and physical center for the political activism I was engaged in at that time.

entity: Society of Professional Engineering Employees in Aerospace
summary: I was a member of the Society of Professional Engineering Employees in Aerospace (SPEEA) during my career at Boeing. This union represented engineers like me and was involved in important labor actions, including the 2000 Boeing engineers strike. SPEEA was a key part of my experience balancing professional work with labor solidarity.
